artifacts.content:
Hi Lisa,

I’m finally sitting down with a mug of chamomile after getting Anna through her math homework and Jonah into bed. I’ve been meaning to actually explain why I’m pouring so much energy into this garden thing behind the library.

You remember how rough May 2022 was when Anna ended up at UW Health’s American Family Children’s Hospital in Madison for those anxiety/panic attacks. It still makes my stomach twist thinking about walking into that unit. Her therapist, Dr. Karen Holtz at Stoughton Hospital Behavioral Health, kept saying over and over that consistent, low‑pressure outdoor time needed to be part of her treatment plan, not just meds and talk therapy. Last summer when we spent 3–4 evenings a week in the little backyard at 214 South Forrest Street messing around with tomatoes and herbs, her panic episodes dropped off so much. Seeing that change is a huge part of why I’m convinced a community garden could help other kids who are quietly struggling.

On a very practical level, it’s also just cheaper to grow at least some food right now. With Eric’s contract at Stoughton High still only at 60% for the 2023–24 year after those April 2023 district cuts, and him picking up random nights at the Stoughton ACE Hardware, we don’t have much wiggle room. We already put about $450 of our own money into lumber, soil, and tools for the trial raised beds in our backyard last summer.

How are things in Sun Prairie? How’s the caseload at the clinic, and is [husband] getting any bites after the GM supplier layoffs? Text me some weekend dates that might work for you all to come see the garden lot once the snow finally melts.

Love,
Rach

metadata.subject: Why I’m so stuck on this garden idea

artifacts.content:
Brain dump – garden program planning (personal)

I keep circling back to why this matters to me beyond the logistics. Part of it is very specific kids.

1) Fox Prairie backyard pilot kid – the one Ms. Danielle Hovland referred after those playground fights in March 2023. He came every Tuesday from July 11 through August 29, 2023, for our little 90‑minute garden group in the backyard at 214 South Forrest. His mom swears the routine plus his anger management sessions at Stoughton Counseling Services cut the outbursts way down. That’s exactly the kind of child I want to build this for.

2) The two 7th graders from Stoughton Middle School who started coming to Wednesday homework club at the Stoughton Public Library after the school social worker sent them my way in March 2023 for truancy/anxiety. Last fall they were hanging out behind the Kwik Trip on East Main after school. Now they drift to homework club and, when weather allows, into the garden space instead. Their parents keep saying it’s a relief.

I have to admit a lot of this is me wanting a sandbox to try program ideas that have been rattling around in my head for years, even if only a handful of kids show up at first. It might never be some huge city‑wide solution, but I need to see if these small, structured outdoor routines can actually move the needle for the kids I already know.

Remember to keep these stories anonymized when I talk about them publicly.

Hi Mom,

Quick update while I eat leftover lentil soup at the reference desk.

First, Dad: I’m still planning to come down to Janesville on Tuesdays and Thursdays, 5:30–9:00 p.m., to help with meds, dishes, and whatever else you need. January’s heart attack and those five days in the cardiac unit at Mercyhealth scared all of us, and I don’t want you carrying everything alone. Keeping the youth garden sessions on Wednesdays from 4:00–5:30 p.m. in Stoughton is actually deliberate so I can block those other evenings for you and Dad.

I’ve also been talking with Paul about summer logistics. He offered to drive you up from Janesville to the garden next to the Stoughton Public Library on Wednesdays June–August 2025 so you can help with basic first‑aid and snack prep. Since you stopped driving after the mild cognitive impairment diagnosis at Mercyhealth back in March 2022, I know it’s been frustrating to depend on rides. I’m trying to set aside about $200 from our program costs so Paul can at least get gas money and an occasional sandwich when he does the round‑trip.

On the kid front, Anna is deep in a Percy Jackson phase and Jonah is still obsessed with the rain barrels. They both want you there this summer to show the other kids how to do “real” bandages the way you did on their stuffed animals.

Call me tonight if you’re not too tired after Dad’s cardiology follow‑up.

Love,
Rachel

Personal planning notes – youth garden + money reality

I need to be honest with myself about capacity. Between 32 hours a week at the Stoughton Public Library, the kids’ schedules at Fox Prairie, and driving to Janesville twice a week for Dad since his heart attack, there just isn’t much left in the tank.

If this little pot of outside funding doesn’t come through, I probably have to shrink the idea back to a few raised beds and informal drop‑ins or even pause it for a year. I just don’t have it in me right now to organize a whole extra round of fundraising events on top of everything else. I can keep volunteering time, but I can’t magic more hours in the day.

There’s also the practical mess from the August 2023 basement flood at 214 South Forrest Street. We lost most of the youth gardening books I’d bought with my own money, plus several boxes of paperwork from when Eric and I set up that IRS payment plan for our 2021 federal taxes. The water turned everything into a moldy lump. Emotionally, I’m still mad about the books; financially, we’re still chipping away at the tax balance.

Note to self: focus energy on making the program lean and sustainable, not perfect. Start small, document what works, and stop assuming I can single‑handedly fix every gap in youth programming.

Hi James,

I wanted to give you a fuller picture of how the garden is intersecting with our existing youth work.

On Wednesdays, I’m seeing a nice overlap between the homework club kids and the garden. The two 7th graders the Stoughton Middle School social worker referred in March 2023 for truancy/anxiety have basically stopped drifting over to the Kwik Trip on East Main after school; they come to the library instead, finish homework, then help water and harvest. Their parents have mentioned fewer attendance calls and fewer evening meltdowns.

We’re also quietly looping in some of Eric’s students. There are two brothers from his 10th‑grade U.S. history class whose dad was arrested for a DUI on County Highway B in May 2023. Last fall they were spending a lot of time roaming the Stoughton Cinema 4 parking lot and getting caught up in minor vandalism that Eric and the VP were tracking. Since they started coming to the garden on Tuesdays and Thursdays, that’s tapered off.

Looking ahead to May 2025, Eric and I are talking about how to safely include three students from his special education social studies section at Stoughton High, including the 10th grader with a history of self‑harm and anxiety who was hospitalized at UW Health in Madison back in April 2023. If we can cover about $300 for adaptive tools and extra supervision, I think the after‑school sessions behind the south parking area could be a really positive, contained space for them.

I realize a lot of this still mostly serves kids who are already connected to the library or the high school. It may take a while before we’re truly reaching families who aren’t already in our orbit, but it feels like a meaningful start.

Best,
Rachel

metadata.subject: How the garden is fitting into our youth work

artifacts.content:
Thread with Kara (children’s librarian)

Rachel: Hey, random thought while I’m labeling plant ID cards – if we end up with a little cushion after buying all the garden supplies, what would you think about using some of it for small thank‑you gift cards for the kids? Like $5 to Kwik Trip or Dairy Queen if they stick with the program all summer. I’m noticing it’s hard to keep some of the middle schoolers engaged.

Kara: Honestly I think that’s smart. Especially for the ones who could be hanging at Cinema 4 or the East Main Kwik Trip instead.

Rachel: Exactly. I’m already seeing that with the two brothers from Eric’s U.S. history class whose dad got the DUI on County B in May 2023 – the garden on Tues/Thurs has kept them out of that Cinema 4 parking lot and the vandalism nonsense Eric and the VP were dealing with last fall.

Kara: Incentives could also help Anna’s crowd.

Rachel: Yeah. I’m hoping to use a tiny bit of the summer 2025 budget to structure volunteer hours for Anna and her friend from Fox Prairie – the one who ended up on juvenile probation after the shoplifting mess at the Stoughton Walmart in October 2024. I’d much rather have them doing supervised tasks in the vacant lot next to the library’s south parking area than wandering downtown.

Kara: 100%. Let me know if you want help tracking hours.

Rachel: Will do. I’ll keep receipts and make sure any extras after tools/seeds/snacks go straight into things that keep kids showing up and feeling valued.

Working doc – garden budget + role

I’m staring at this spreadsheet and realizing I still don’t have exact prices for everything. I’ve pulled rough numbers for cedar beds, soil/compost, drip irrigation, youth tools, snacks, bilingual signage, etc., based on what we spent on last year’s summer reading build‑a‑garden craft series, but this is still more educated guess than precise estimate. I’ll need to firm it up with actual quotes from Stoughton Garden Center and the print shop once I have bandwidth.

Big line items:
– 10 cedar raised beds (including 2 tall ones for kids with mobility challenges)
– Soil/compost delivery (about 8 cu yd soil, 4 cu yd compost)
– Drip irrigation + rain barrels
– Youth‑sized tools, gloves, lockable storage box
– Seeds, starter plants, pollinator bed
– Garden journals, laminated plant ID cards, English/Spanish signs
– Healthy snacks, sunscreen, insect repellent, first‑aid kit

I’m also trying to be realistic about what this project can and can’t do. It’s not going to solve housing or transportation issues in Stoughton. It’s a small, nice‑to‑have enrichment space that turns an ugly vacant lot next to the library’s south parking area into something alive and kid‑friendly. That’s still worth doing.

Remember: track every expense line‑by‑line so Paul can reconcile the books easily.

From: rachel.lindstrom@stoughtonlibrary.org
To: foxprairie.pta.president@gmail.com
Date: May 20, 2024
Subject: Coordinating youth garden produce with pantry families

Hi Megan,

I’ve been meaning to loop the Fox Prairie Elementary PTA in on something I’ve been doing more informally this spring.

Since April 2024, I’ve been setting aside about 10–15 pounds of kid‑friendly produce each week from the youth garden behind the Stoughton Public Library and sending it straight to the Stoughton Food Pantry. I’ve been coordinating quietly with Ms. Jensen from Anna’s 5th‑grade class so that at least some of that goes to families we already know are on the PTA grocery gift card list and who also rely on the free Wednesday meal at Covenant Lutheran Church in Stoughton.

Because I know the specific families’ names from the Fox Prairie Elementary PTA records, I’ve been able to prioritize familiar veggies their kids helped plant—cucumbers, cherry tomatoes, snap peas—so when they show up at the pantry’s fresh produce section, the kids can literally point and say, “I grew that.” It’s been a small but meaningful way to stretch grocery budgets without adding any stigma.

I want to be sure we’re handling this thoughtfully and in line with PTA expectations around privacy and equity. Would you be open to a quick meeting—maybe after school one day next week—to talk about whether we should formalize this, or keep it as a quiet, targeted support for now?

Thanks for all you do,
Rachel

—
Rachel Marie Lindstrom
Youth Services Librarian & Community Program Coordinator
Stoughton Public Library

metadata.subject: Coordinating youth garden produce with pantry families

artifacts.content:
From: rachel.lindstrom@stoughtonlibrary.org
To: lisa.pt@gmail.com
Date: June 18, 2024
Subject: Why this garden season feels so personal

Lisa,

I was thinking of you and wanted to explain why I’m pouring so much into the garden behind the Stoughton Public Library this year.

You’d love seeing Marjorie out there. She’s the 79‑year‑old widow from the Stoughton Senior Center I started visiting monthly after her mild stroke at Stoughton Hospital back in February 2022. Remember how depressed and housebound she was for months? Now she comes over with the Senior Center van twice a week, standing in the beds showing the kids how her family used to plant potatoes. Those monthly home visits since 2022 turned into garden “grandparent” shifts, and you can see her confidence coming back.

On the family side, I keep thinking about you and [husband] since his layoff from the GM supplier plant in Janesville in March 2024, and how Mom and Dad (Carolyn and Mark) have been quietly helping cover part of your medical school loan payments. Seeing how our little shared plots can reliably give families about $20–$30 worth of produce per week has made me realize this isn’t just a cute kid project—it’s a concrete budget tool. Mom told me she’s been sharing our numbers with her group at St. John Vianney Catholic Church in Janesville so other Rock County families in the same boat can copy the model.

If your Janesville church friends want details, I can send a simple “how‑to” with our weekly harvest breakdown.

Love,
Rachel

metadata.subject: Why this garden season feels so personal

artifacts.content:
From: rachel.lindstrom@stoughtonlibrary.org
To: katie.h@mononalibrary.org
Date: March 4, 2024
Subject: Outdoor programming + risk management lessons from our garden

Hi Katie,

Following up on your question about safety for outdoor youth programs, I’ll be honest: my caution comes from a hard‑earned lesson.

Back in September 2021, during an outdoor storytime in the small green space behind Stoughton Public Library, a 4‑year‑old tripped over a folding chair. His parents filed an incident report with the City of Stoughton, and I ended up receiving a written warning from the City of Stoughton risk management office. No one was seriously hurt, but it triggered a full review with our library director, James Keller, and the city’s insurance representative at the municipal offices. We tightened everything—site diagrams, incident documentation, staff/child ratios, even how we position chairs.

That experience is a big reason I’m so meticulous now with the youth garden. I’m building in clear consent forms, emergency contacts, and behavior expectations; documenting that some of our volunteers are more vulnerable (e.g., Marjorie from the Stoughton Senior Center, who is still rebuilding strength after her mild stroke at Stoughton Hospital in 2022); and making sure tools, hoses, and raised beds are arranged to minimize trip‑and‑fall risks.

For your new courtyard program, I’d suggest doing a quick mock “risk walk‑through” with whoever handles insurance for Monona, and keeping any past incidents (like my 2021 warning) in mind as cautionary tales without putting them front‑and‑center in public‑facing materials.

Happy to share our checklists if you’d like.

Best,
Rachel
